title: Find lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two nodes
description: Find the lowest common ancestor of two given nodes in a general binary tree

examples.explanation: Step 1: Find path from root (3) to node 5. DFS: Start at 3, go left to 5 (path: [3,5]). Step 2: Find path from root to node 1. DFS: Start at 3, go right to 1 (path: [3,1]). Step 3: Compare paths: path1=[3,5], path2=[3,1]. Iterate i=0: both 3 (match). i=1: 5 vs 1 (mismatch). Last common node at i=0 is 3. Output: 3.

Step 1: Find path to 5: [3,5]. Step 2: Find path to 4: DFS from 3: go left to 5, then right to 2, then left to 7, right to 4 (path: [3,5,2,7,4]). Step 3: Compare: path1=[3,5], path2=[3,5,2,7,4]. i=0: 3 match, i=1: 5 match, i=2: path1 has no element (end). Last common node at i=1 is 5. Output: 5.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Path Comparison O(n²)
approaches.summary: Find paths from root to each node by traversing the tree, then compare the paths to find the last common node. This is straightforward but inefficient for large trees.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Path Comparison for LCA
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of finding LCA by comparing paths from root to both nodes.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the LCA problem by first finding the paths from the root to each target node, then comparing these paths to identify their last common node. The intuition is straightforward: the LCA is the deepest node that appears in both paths before they diverge. We perform a DFS traversal to find each path, storing nodes along the way. Once both paths are obtained, we iterate through them simultaneously until we find a mismatch; the previous node is the LCA. This method is conceptually simple but inefficient for large trees due to repeated traversals and path comparisons. The time complexity is O(n²) in the worst case because we might traverse the entire tree twice (once per node) and compare paths of length up to n. Space complexity is O(n) for storing paths and recursion stack. This connects directly to L1 by using the path-finding subroutine as a building block. Common mistakes include not handling cases where one node is an ancestor of the other (the LCA should be that ancestor), or assuming the tree is a BST (it's a general binary tree). Real code pattern involves recursive DFS with backtracking to build paths.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find path from root (3) to node 5. DFS: Start at 3, go left to 5 (path: [3,5]). Step 2: Find path from root to node 1. DFS: Start at 3, go right to 1 (path: [3,1]). Step 3: Compare paths: path1=[3,5], path2=[3,1]. Iterate i=0: both 3 (match). i=1: 5 vs 1 (mismatch). Last common node at i=0 is 3. Output: 3.

Step 1: Find path to 5: [3,5]. Step 2: Find path to 4: DFS from 3: go left to 5, then right to 2, then left to 7, right to 4 (path: [3,5,2,7,4]). Step 3: Compare: path1=[3,5], path2=[3,5,2,7,4]. i=0: 3 match, i=1: 5 match, i=2: path1 has no element (end). Last common node at i=1 is 5. Output: 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Path from Root to Target Node
ladders.desc: DFS traversal to collect the path from root to a given node in a binary tree.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for finding the sequence of nodes from the root to a target node. The intuition is to perform a depth-first search (DFS) recursively, backtracking to build the path only if the target is found. We start at the root and explore each branch deeply before moving to the next. If the current node matches the target, we add it to the path and return True. Otherwise, we recursively search left and right subtrees; if either returns True, we add the current node to the path (since it's part of the path to the target). The algorithm uses a list to store the path in reverse order (from target to root) during backtracking, then reverses it for the final path. Time complexity is O(n) in the worst case as we may traverse all nodes. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and path storage. This feeds into L0 by providing the paths needed for comparison. Common mistakes include not handling null nodes properly or forgetting to reverse the path. Real code pattern involves recursive DFS with a helper function that returns a boolean.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 1, not target. Step 2: Go left to 2, not target. Step 3: Go left to 4, not target, both children null, return False. Step 4: Back to 2, go right to 5, is target, add 5 to path, return True. Step 5: Back to 2, since right True, add 2 to path, return True. Step 6: Back to 1, since left True, add 1 to path, return True. Path built: [5,2,1], reverse to [1,2,5].

Step 1: Start at 1, not target. Step 2: Go left to 2, not target, both children null, return False. Step 3: Back to 1, go right to 3, is target, add 3 to path, return True. Step 4: Back to 1, since right True, add 1 to path, return True. Path: [3,1], reverse to [1,3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compare Two Paths to Find Last Common Node
ladders.desc: Iterate through two lists to identify the last matching element before they diverge.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique in the brute force approach: once we have two paths (from root to each node), we compare them element by element to find the last common node. The intuition is that the LCA is the deepest node where the paths overlap; after this point, the paths split to reach their respective targets. We iterate through both paths simultaneously using an index, comparing nodes at each position. As long as they match, we update the LCA candidate. When a mismatch occurs or one path ends, the last matched node is the LCA. This works because paths are ordered from root to target, so common ancestors appear in sequence. The algorithm is simple: initialize LCA as null, loop while within bounds of both paths, if nodes match set LCA to that node, else break. Time complexity is O(min(len(path1), len(path2))) which is O(n) in worst case. Space complexity is O(1) aside from input paths. This connects to L1 by using the paths generated from the sub-routine. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop or not handling cases where one path is a prefix of the other (LCA is the last node of the shorter path). Real code pattern involves a while loop with index comparison.

examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, node1=1, node2=1, match, LCA=1. Step 2: i=1, node1=2, node2=3, mismatch, break. Output: 1.
Step 1: i=0, 1 vs 1, match, LCA=1. Step 2: i=1, 2 vs 2, match, LCA=2. Step 3: i=2, 4 vs 5, mismatch, break. Output: 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — DFS Traversal with Backtracking in Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Perform depth-first search to explore all nodes, with ability to backtrack and collect data.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential for finding paths in a tree. DFS traversal involves exploring as far down a branch as possible before backtracking to explore other branches. In a binary tree, we recursively visit the left subtree, then the right subtree. Backtracking allows us to return to parent nodes and collect information, such as building a path when a target is found. The intuition is that recursion naturally implements backtracking: when a recursive call returns, we go back to the caller (the parent node). We use a list to store nodes during the traversal, adding nodes when moving down and removing them when backtracking if the target isn't found, or keeping them if it is. This is key for L2 because it enables path collection. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack. Common mistakes include infinite recursion if base cases are missing, or incorrect order of operations. Real code pattern uses a recursive function that takes a node and a path list.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3], collect all nodes via DFS
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at 1, add to path: [1]. Step 2: Recurse left to 2, add: [1,2]. Step 3: 2 has no left/right, return, path stays. Step 4: Back to 1, recurse right to 3, add: [1,2,3]. Step 5: 3 has no children, return. Final path: [1,2,3].

Step 1: At 1, not target, go left to 2. Step 2: At 2, not target, go left to 4. Step 3: At 4, is target, add to path and return True. Step 4: Back to 2, since left True, add 2 to path. Step 5: Back to 1, since left True, add 1 to path. Path: [4,2,1], reverse to [1,2,4].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Traverse Binary Tree Node with Left/Right Children
ladders.desc: Access and move to left and right children of a node in a binary tree.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves navigating a binary tree by visiting a node and then proceeding to its left or right child. In a binary tree, each node has up to two children: left and right. Traversal means starting at a node (e.g., the root), checking if it has a left child, and if so, moving to that child, and similarly for the right child. This is basic to tree data structures and is used in all tree algorithms. The intuition is that trees are hierarchical, and traversal allows us to explore the structure. We typically use pointers or references: node.left gives the left child, node.right gives the right child. If a child is null, it means no node exists in that direction. This operation is crucial for L3 as it enables DFS traversal. Time complexity is O(1) per access. Space complexity is O(1). Common mistakes include accessing children without null checks, leading to errors. Real code pattern uses if statements to check for null before recursing.

examples.input: Node with value 1, left=2, right=3, traverse from 1 to left
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 1. Step 2: Check left child: exists (node 2). Step 3: Move to left child, now at node 2.

examples.input: Node with value 1, left=null, right=3, traverse from 1 to right
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at node 1. Step 2: Check left child: null, so skip. Step 3: Check right child: exists (node 3). Step 4: Move to right child, now at node 3.

testCases.input: Node: val=1, left=2, right=3, go left
Node: val=1, left=2, right=3, go right
Node: val=1, left=null, right=3, go left
Node: val=1, left=2, right=null, go right
Node: val=1, left=null, right=null, go left
Node: val=1, left=2, right=3, go left then right from 2
Node: val=1, left=2, right=3, go right then left from 3
Node: val=1, left=2, right=3, check left exists
Node: val=1, left=null, right=3, check left exists
Node: val=1, left=2, right=3, check right exists

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Properties
ladders.desc: Understand what a binary tree node is and its basic attributes.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a binary tree node is a fundamental data structure unit that forms a binary tree. Each node contains a value (e.g., an integer) and references to up to two other nodes: a left child and a right child. The tree starts with a root node, and nodes are connected such that each node has at most two children, forming a hierarchical structure. Key properties: nodes can be null (indicating no child), the root has no parent, and leaf nodes have no children. This is foundational because all tree algorithms, including LCA, operate on these nodes. The intuition is that trees represent relationships, like family trees, where each node can branch into two. In code, a node is typically defined as a class with attributes for value, left, and right. This concept feeds into L4 by providing the objects we traverse. Common mistakes include confusing nodes with values or not understanding null references. Real code pattern involves defining a class TreeNode.

examples.input: Define a node with value 5, left child null, right child null
examples.trace: Step 1: Create a node object. Step 2: Set its value to 5. Step 3: Set left reference to null. Step 4: Set right reference to null. This represents a leaf node.

examples.input: Define a node with value 3, left child node 1, right child node 6
examples.trace: Step 1: Create node for value 3. Step 2: Create node for value 1. Step 3: Create node for value 6. Step 4: Set left of node 3 to node 1. Step 5: Set right of node 3 to node 6. This forms a small tree.

testCases.expected: true if left and right null

approaches.name: Approach 2: Recursive Post-Order Traversal O(n)
approaches.summary: Traverse the tree recursively, returning the node if it matches either target or if both subtrees return non-null, indicating the LCA. This avoids storing paths and uses a single pass.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is tree height

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find LCA via Recursive Post-Order Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find lowest common ancestor of two nodes in a binary tree using single-pass recursion.
ladders.explanation: This approach finds the LCA without storing paths by performing a single post-order traversal. The intuition is that the LCA is the first node where both target nodes appear in different subtrees, or the node itself matches one target while the other appears in its subtree. We recursively traverse left and right subtrees, returning the node if it matches either target, or returning the node when both subtrees return non-null values. This works because in post-order traversal, we process children before parent, allowing us to propagate information upward. The algorithm visits each node once, making it O(n) time. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack, where h is tree height. This connects to L1 as the complete solution using the recursive helper. Common mistakes include not handling null returns properly, confusing pre-order with post-order, and not considering cases where one node is ancestor of the other. Real code pattern: define recursive function that returns node if found, else null; at each node, check left/right results; if both non-null, current node is LCA; if one non-null and current matches other target, current is LCA; otherwise propagate non-null result upward.

examples.trace:
1. Start at root 3: call left(5), right(1)
2. At node 5: left(6) returns null, right(2) returns 2 (found q=1 in subtree)
3. At node 2: left(7) returns null, right(4) returns null, node 2 ≠ p/q → return null
4. Back to 5: left null, right null, node 5 = p → return 5
5. At node 1: left(0) returns null, right(8) returns null, node 1 = q → return 1
6. Back to root 3: left returns 5, right returns 1 → both non-null → LCA = 3

1. Start at root 3: left(5), right(1)
2. At node 5: left(6) returns null, right(2) returns 2 (found q=4 in subtree)
3. At node 2: left(7) returns null, right(4) returns 4 (node matches q)
4. Back to 5: left null, right returns 2, node 5 = p → LCA = 5 (since one subtree has q and node is p)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive LCA Finder Function
ladders.desc: The recursive function that traverses tree and returns LCA information.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that implements the LCA logic. The intuition is that each recursive call returns either: the target node if found in that subtree, the LCA if discovered, or null if neither target is in that subtree. We use post-order traversal (left, right, then current) because we need information from both subtrees before deciding at the current node. At each node: 1) Recursively call left and right. 2) If current node matches p or q, return current node. 3) If left and right both return non-null, current node is LCA. 4) If only one side returns non-null, propagate that value upward. 5) If both null, return null. Time complexity is O(n) as each node visited once. Space is O(h) for recursion stack. This feeds into L0 as the main algorithm component. Common mistakes: returning too early before checking both subtrees, not handling null cases correctly, confusing traversal order. Real pattern: function findLCA(node, p, q): if node null → return null; left = findLCA(node.left); right = findLCA(node.right); if node = p or q → return node; if left and right non-null → return node; return left or right (whichever non-null).

examples.trace:
1. Node 1: left(2), right(3)
2. Node 2: left null, right null, node=2=p → return 2
3. Node 3: left null, right null, node=3=q → return 3
4. Back to 1: left=2, right=3 → both non-null → return 1 as LCA

1. Node 1: left(2), right null
2. Node 2: left(3), right null
3. Node 3: left null, right null, node=3=p → return 3
4. Back to 2: left=3, right null, node=2=q → return 2 (since node matches q and left has p)
5. Back to 1: left=2, right null → return 2

ladders.title: Core Logic — Post-Order Traversal with Information Propagation
ladders.desc: The technique of using post-order traversal to propagate found nodes upward to find LCA.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: using post-order traversal (left, right, current) allows information about found nodes to flow upward from leaves to root, enabling LCA detection without extra storage. The intuition is that to identify an LCA, we need to know if p and q exist in the current node's subtrees. By processing children first, each node receives 'reports' from its left and right subtrees about whether they contain p or q. If both subtrees report finding targets, current node is LCA. If only one subtree reports a target, we propagate that information upward. If current node itself is a target, we report it upward. This creates a bottom-up information flow perfect for LCA discovery. Time O(n), space O(h). This feeds into L1 as the traversal strategy. Common mistakes: using pre-order instead (wouldn't have subtree info), not propagating single found nodes correctly. Real pattern: traverse left, then right, then process current node combining results.

examples.trace:
1. Leaf 2: returns self (found p)
2. Leaf 3: returns self (found q)
3. Node 1: receives 2 from left, 3 from right → identifies as LCA

1. Leaf 3: returns self (found p)
2. Node 2: receives 3 from left, null from right, self matches q → returns self
3. Node 1: receives 2 from left, null from right → propagates 2 upward

testCases.input: Tree: [1,2,3,4], propagation at node 2 with left=4, right=null
Tree: [1,2,3,4], propagation at node 1 with left=2, right=3
Tree: [1,2,3,4,5], propagation at node 2 with left=4, right=5
Tree: [1,2,3,4,5], propagation at node 1 with left=2, right=null
Tree: [1], propagation at node 1 with left=null, right=null
Tree: [1,2,3,4,5,6], propagation at node 2 with left=4, right=null

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Tree Recursive Traversal
ladders.desc: Implementing recursive traversal of binary trees to visit all nodes.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a binary tree recursively to visit every node. The intuition is that trees have recursive structure: each node has left and right subtrees that are themselves trees. We can traverse by: visiting the node, recursively traversing left subtree, then right subtree (pre-order); or left, right, then node (post-order); or left, node, right (in-order). For LCA, we use post-order. The algorithm: function traverse(node): if node null → return; traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right); process node. This ensures all nodes are visited exactly once. Time O(n), space O(h). This feeds into L2 as the traversal mechanism. Common mistakes: infinite recursion on null nodes, wrong traversal order, not understanding recursion depth. Real pattern: define recursive function with base case (node null), recursive calls on left/right, then process current node.

examples.trace:
1. Start at 1: call left(2)
2. At 2: left null, right null, process 2
3. Back to 1: call right(3)
4. At 3: left null, right null, process 3
5. Back to 1: process 1

1. Start at 1: left(2)
2. At 2: left(3)
3. At 3: left null, right null, process 3
4. Back to 2: right null, process 2
5. Back to 1: right null, process 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Node Comparison and Null Checking
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of comparing nodes and handling null values in tree traversal.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves two key skills: 1) Comparing tree nodes to check if they match target nodes, and 2) Properly handling null nodes (empty subtrees). The intuition is that tree traversal requires checking each node against targets, and recursion must stop at null nodes to avoid errors. For comparison, we check if current node equals p or q (by reference or value). For null handling, we return immediately when node is null, as empty subtree contains no targets. These operations are basic building blocks for any tree algorithm. Time O(1) per node, space O(1). This feeds into L3 as essential components. Common mistakes: comparing nodes incorrectly (should compare references/values based on problem), not checking for null before accessing node properties, infinite recursion by not handling null. Real pattern: if node is null: return null; if node == p or node == q: return node.

examples.trace:
1. Check node null? No
2. Compare node with p: 5==5 → true
3. Compare node with q: 5==3 → false
4. Result: node matches p

1. Check node null? Yes
2. Return null immediately (no further operations)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic structure of a binary tree node.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding what a binary tree node is. A binary tree node is a data structure that contains: 1) A value (integer, string, etc.), 2) A reference/pointer to left child node, 3) A reference/pointer to right child node. The intuition is that each node can have at most two children, forming a hierarchical structure. The left and right references can be null, indicating no child in that direction. This structure enables recursive algorithms because each node's children are themselves trees. Time/space not applicable as this is data structure definition. This feeds into L4 as the object we operate on. Common mistakes: confusing nodes with values, not understanding null children, thinking trees are always balanced. Real pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None.

examples.trace:
1. Create node with value 1
2. Create left child node with value 2
3. Create right child node with value 3
4. Set node.left = left child, node.right = right child

1. Create node with value 5
2. left is None (no left child)
3. right is None (no right child)

approaches.name: Approach 3: Iterative with Parent Pointers O(n)
approaches.summary: First traverse to build a map of parent pointers for each node, then trace ancestors from the two nodes upwards to find the first common one. This is useful if parent pointers are not given.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find LCA Using Iterative Parent Pointer Approach
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find lowest common ancestor by building parent map and tracing ancestors upward
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the LCA problem in a general binary tree without parent pointers by first building a parent pointer map through traversal, then tracing ancestors from both nodes upward until finding the first common ancestor. The intuition is that we can simulate having parent pointers by creating them ourselves through a traversal. First, we perform any tree traversal (BFS or DFS) to record each node's parent in a hash map. Once we have this map, we start from node p and trace all its ancestors upward to the root, storing them in a set. Then we start from node q and move upward, checking at each step if the current ancestor exists in p's ancestor set. The first match is the LCA. This works because the LCA is the deepest node that's an ancestor to both p and q, so by comparing ancestor chains from both nodes, we find their intersection point. The algorithm: 1. If root is null or p/q is root, return root. 2. Create parent map via BFS/DFS. 3. Create set of p's ancestors by tracing upward from p using parent map. 4. Trace upward from q, checking each ancestor against p's set; return first match. Time: O(n) for traversal and ancestor tracing. Space: O(n) for parent map and ancestor set. This feeds into the full problem by combining traversal, parent mapping, and ancestor comparison. Common mistakes: not handling null inputs, forgetting to include p itself in ancestor set, infinite loops if tree has cycles. Real code pattern: def lca(root, p, q): if not root or root == p or root == q: return root; parent = {}; stack = [root]; while stack: node = stack.pop(); if node.left: parent[node.left] = node; stack.append(node.left); if node.right: parent[node.right] = node; stack.append(node.right); ancestors = set(); while p: ancestors.add(p); p = parent.get(p); while q: if q in ancestors: return q; q = parent.get(q); return None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build parent map via DFS. Start at root 3, parent map empty. Process 3: add left child 5 with parent 3, right child 1 with parent 3. Stack: [5,1]. Process 1: add left child 0 with parent 1, right child 8 with parent 1. Stack: [5,0,8]. Process 8: no children. Stack: [5,0]. Process 0: no children. Stack: [5]. Process 5: add left child 6 with parent 5, right child 2 with parent 5. Stack: [6,2]. Process 2: add left child 7 with parent 2, right child 4 with parent 2. Stack: [6,7,4]. Process 4,7,6: no children. Parent map complete. Step 2: Trace p=5 ancestors: {5}. Get parent of 5: 3, add to set: {5,3}. Parent of 3: None, stop. Step 3: Trace q=1 ancestors: Check 1 in {5,3}? No. Get parent of 1: 3, check 3 in {5,3}? Yes. Return 3 as LCA.

Step 1: Build parent map via DFS. Start at root 1, parent map empty. Process 1: add left child 2 with parent 1, right child 3 with parent 1. Stack: [2,3]. Process 3: no children. Stack: [2]. Process 2: add right child 4 with parent 2. Stack: [4]. Process 4: no children. Parent map complete. Step 2: Trace p=4 ancestors: {4}. Get parent of 4: 2, add to set: {4,2}. Get parent of 2: 1, add to set: {4,2,1}. Parent of 1: None, stop. Step 3: Trace q=3 ancestors: Check 3 in {4,2,1}? No. Get parent of 3: 1, check 1 in {4,2,1}? Yes. Return 1 as LCA.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Build Parent Pointer Map via Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Traverse binary tree to create mapping from each node to its parent
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the first critical step in the LCA approach: building a parent pointer map through tree traversal. The intuition is that we need to know each node's parent to trace ancestor chains upward, but binary trees typically don't store parent pointers. By traversing the tree (DFS or BFS), whenever we visit a node, we record its children's parent as the current node. This creates a complete parent map for all nodes except the root (which has no parent). The algorithm: 1. Initialize empty parent dictionary. 2. Start traversal from root. 3. For each node visited: if it has left child, map left child → current node; if it has right child, map right child → current node. 4. Continue until all nodes visited. We can use DFS (stack) or BFS (queue). Time: O(n) visiting each node once. Space: O(n) for parent map. This feeds into L0 by providing the parent map needed for ancestor tracing. Common mistakes: not handling null root, forgetting to map both children, infinite recursion in cycles. Real code pattern: def build_parent(root): if not root: return {}; parent = {}; stack = [root]; while stack: node = stack.pop(); if node.left: parent[node.left] = node; stack.append(node.left); if node.right: parent[node.right] = node; stack.append(node.right); return parent.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1, parent map empty, stack [1]. Step 2: Pop 1, process: left child 2 exists, add parent[2]=1; right child 3 exists, add parent[3]=1. Push children: stack [2,3]. Step 3: Pop 3, no children. Stack [2]. Step 4: Pop 2, no children. Stack empty. Final parent map: {2:1, 3:1}.

Step 1: Start at root 1, parent map empty, stack [1]. Step 2: Pop 1, process: left child 2 exists, add parent[2]=1; right child null. Push left: stack [2]. Step 3: Pop 2, process: left child 3 exists, add parent[3]=2; right child 4 exists, add parent[4]=2. Push children: stack [3,4]. Step 4: Pop 4, no children. Stack [3]. Step 5: Pop 3, no children. Stack empty. Final parent map: {2:1, 3:2, 4:2}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Trace Ancestors Upward Using Parent Map
ladders.desc: Given parent map and two nodes, find first common ancestor by tracing upward
ladders.explanation: This is the core logic that finds the LCA once we have parent pointers. The intuition is that the LCA is the first node where the ancestor chains from p and q intersect when traced upward toward the root. We first collect all ancestors of p (including p itself) in a set for O(1) lookup. Then we trace q's ancestors upward, checking each one against p's ancestor set. The first match is the LCA because we're moving upward from q, so we encounter deeper ancestors first. The algorithm: 1. Create empty set for p's ancestors. 2. Starting from p, add p to set, then move to its parent (using parent map), add parent, continue until reaching null (root's parent). 3. Starting from q, check if q is in p's ancestor set; if yes, return q. 4. Otherwise move to q's parent, check again, continue until finding match or reaching null. Time: O(h) where h is tree height, worst case O(n). Space: O(h) for ancestor set. This feeds into L1 by using the parent map to perform the actual LCA finding. Common mistakes: forgetting to include p itself in ancestor set, not handling case where p is ancestor of q or vice versa, infinite loop if parent map has cycles. Real code pattern: def find_lca_from_parent(parent, p, q): ancestors = set(); while p: ancestors.add(p); p = parent.get(p); while q: if q in ancestors: return q; q = parent.get(q); return None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build p's ancestors set. Start p=4, add 4 to set: {4}. Get parent of 4: 2, add 2: {4,2}. Get parent of 2: 1, add 1: {4,2,1}. Get parent of 1: None (not in map), stop. Step 2: Trace q upward. Start q=5, check 5 in {4,2,1}? No. Get parent of 5: 2, check 2 in {4,2,1}? Yes. Return 2 as LCA.

Step 1: Build p's ancestors set. Start p=2, add 2 to set: {2}. Get parent of 2: 1, add 1: {2,1}. Get parent of 1: None, stop. Step 2: Trace q upward. Start q=3, check 3 in {2,1}? No. Get parent of 3: 1, check 1 in {2,1}? Yes. Return 1 as LCA.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal (DFS/BFS) to Visit All Nodes
ladders.desc: Visit every node in a binary tree using depth-first or breadth-first search
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed to build the parent map: traversing the entire tree to visit each node. The intuition is that to record parent-child relationships, we need to visit every node and process its children. We can use DFS (depth-first search) with a stack or BFS (breadth-first search) with a queue. DFS is often simpler for this task. The algorithm for iterative DFS: 1. Initialize stack with root. 2. While stack not empty: pop node, process it (e.g., record its children's parent), push right child then left child (for pre-order). For BFS: use queue, dequeue node, process, enqueue children. Time: O(n) visiting n nodes. Space: O(h) for DFS stack, O(w) for BFS queue where h is height, w is max width. This feeds into L2 by providing the mechanism to build the parent map. Common mistakes: forgetting to check for null children before pushing, infinite loops in cyclic structures, stack overflow in deep trees. Real code pattern: def dfs_traversal(root): if not root: return; stack = [root]; while stack: node = stack.pop(); # Process node here if node.right: stack.append(node.right); if node.left: stack.append(node.left).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start stack [1]. Step 2: Pop 1, visit 1. Push right child 3, left child 2: stack [3,2]. Step 3: Pop 2, visit 2. No children. Stack [3]. Step 4: Pop 3, visit 3. No children. Stack empty. Visited order: 1, 2, 3 (if push left then right) or 1, 3, 2 (if push right then left).

examples.output: Visited nodes: 1, 3, 2, 5, 4 (DFS pre-order, push right then left)
examples.trace: Step 1: Stack [1]. Pop 1, visit 1. Push right 3, left 2: stack [3,2]. Step 2: Pop 2, visit 2. Push right 5, left 4: stack [3,5,4]. Step 3: Pop 4, visit 4. No children. Stack [3,5]. Step 4: Pop 5, visit 5. No children. Stack [3]. Step 5: Pop 3, visit 3. No children. Stack empty. Visited: 1,2,4,5,3 if push left then right.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Node Children and Store in Hash Map
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation: check if node has children and record parent-child relationship in dictionary
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is used repeatedly during tree traversal: for each node visited, check its left and right children, and if they exist, store the relationship in a hash map (dictionary). The intuition is that we're building a reverse mapping from child to parent. The operation involves: 1. Checking if node.left is not null. 2. If yes, map node.left → node in dictionary. 3. Checking if node.right is not null. 4. If yes, map node.right → node. This is done for every node during traversal. The hash map provides O(1) access to any node's parent later. Time: O(1) per node. Space: O(1) per child stored. This feeds into L3 by being the core processing step during traversal. Common mistakes: not checking for null before accessing children (causing null pointer errors), overwriting existing mappings incorrectly, using wrong dictionary key-value order. Real code pattern: parent = {}; if node.left: parent[node.left] = node; if node.right: parent[node.right] = node.

examples.input: Node with value 1, left=2, right=3, parent map initially empty
examples.trace: Step 1: Check node.left: exists (node with value 2). Add parent[2] = 1. Step 2: Check node.right: exists (node with value 3). Add parent[3] = 1. Final parent map: {2:1, 3:1}.

examples.input: Node with value 2, left=4, right=null, parent map currently {2:1}
examples.trace: Step 1: Check node.left: exists (node with value 4). Add parent[4] = 2. Step 2: Check node.right: null, do nothing. Final parent map: {2:1, 4:2}.

testCases.input: Node: value=1, left=null, right=null, parent map={}
Node: value=1, left=2, right=null, parent map={}
Node: value=1, left=null, right=3, parent map={}
Node: value=1, left=2, right=3, parent map={}
Node: value=2, left=4, right=5, parent map={2:1}
Node: value=3, left=6, right=7, parent map={3:1}
Node: value=4, left=null, right=null, parent map={4:2}
Node: value=5, left=8, right=9, parent map={5:2}
Node: value=6, left=10, right=null, parent map={6:3}
Node: value=7, left=null, right=11, parent map={7:3}

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Parent-Child Relationship
ladders.desc: Understanding basic binary tree node with left/right pointers and parent-child hierarchy
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what a binary tree node is and the parent-child relationship. A binary tree node typically has: a value (integer, string, etc.), a left child pointer, and a right child pointer. The parent-child relationship is directional: a parent node points to its children via left/right pointers, but children don't point back to parent (unless it's a special tree with parent pointers). The root is the topmost node with no parent. Leaves are nodes with no children. The intuition is that trees are hierarchical structures where each node (except root) has exactly one parent, but can have 0, 1, or 2 children. This is foundational because to find LCA, we need to understand ancestor chains: parent of a node, grandparent, etc. For example, if node A is parent of node B, and B is parent of C, then A is ancestor of C. Time/space: not applicable for single node. This feeds into L4 by defining what we're operating on. Common mistakes: confusing parent-child direction, thinking trees are always balanced, not understanding null pointers represent absent children. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None.

examples.input: Simple tree: root with value 1, left child with value 2, right child with value 3
examples.output: Parent-child: 1 is parent of 2 and 3; 2 and 3 are children of 1
examples.trace: Node 1: left pointer points to node 2, right pointer points to node 3. Node 2: left and right pointers are null (no children). Node 3: left and right pointers are null. So parent-child relationships: 1 → 2, 1 → 3. 2 has parent 1, 3 has parent 1.

examples.input: Tree: root 1, left child 2, left-left child 3
examples.output: Parent-child: 1 is parent of 2; 2 is parent of 3
examples.trace: Node 1: left points to node 2, right null. Node 2: left points to node 3, right null. Node 3: left null, right null. So relationships: 1 → 2, 2 → 3. 2 has parent 1, 3 has parent 2. Ancestor chain: 1 is ancestor of 3.

testCases.input: Node 1 with left child 2 and right child 3
Node 2 with left child 4, where 2 is left child of 1
testCases.expected: 1 is parent of 2, 2 is parent of 4

testCases.input: Node 3 with right child 6, where 3 is right child of 1
testCases.expected: 1 is parent of 3, 3 is parent of 6

testCases.input: Node 4 with no children, where 4 is left child of 2
Node 5 with left child 10 and right child 11
Node 6 with left child 12, where 6 is right child of 3
testCases.expected: 3 is parent of 6, 6 is parent of 12

testCases.input: Node 7 with no children, where 7 is left child of 4
